planting.note: Start indoors under lights and plant out once soil is warm.

directions: Cage or stake this vigorous indeterminate and prune to keep airflow around the big fruit. The pink-red hearts can top a pound and hold few seeds in their sweet, solid flesh. Pick as the fruit colors up fully and yields to a soft squeeze.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Set plants deep and support the tall vines as the heart-shaped fruit develops. Keep soil moisture steady to reduce cracking and blossom-end rot on the large fruit. Harvest when the fruit turns a rich golden yellow and softens at the pointed base.

planting.note: Start seed early indoors and move plants outside after frost.

directions: Transplant deeply and stake the vigorous vines, which set large heart-shaped fruit over a long season. Mulch and water evenly to keep the meaty flesh from splitting. Pick when the fruit ripens to a soft rose-pink and feels dense in the hand.

planting.note: Sow indoors and set out hardened transplants after frost danger.

directions: Stake this tall indeterminate and prune suckers to help it size up the big pink oxheart fruit. Feed regularly and water steadily for the sweet, low-acid flesh to fill out. Harvest when the heart-shaped fruit colors to a deep pink and gives slightly.

planting.note: Begin indoors and transplant after nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Plant deeply and give the tall vines a strong stake or cage for the heavy hearts. Keep moisture even and mulch to protect the dense, meaty fruit from cracking. Pick when the fruit ripens to a bright red and softens near the pointed tip.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out once frost has passed.

directions: This is the classic large heart-shaped heirloom; transplant deep and support the tall vines. Water consistently and mulch to keep the big, low-seed fruit from splitting. Gather the meaty pink-red hearts as they color fully and soften slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out plants after the last frost.

directions: Support this Tuscan indeterminate on a stake and thin suckers for larger fruit. The flattened, strongly pleated fruit is bright red with dense, flavorful flesh for cooking. Harvest when the ribbed shoulders color evenly and the fruit softens.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and transplant once the soil warms.

directions: Grow this Mexican heirloom on a stake in full sun and heat for best set. The pink-red fruit is deeply pleated and hollow, making it a natural for stuffing. Pick when the accordion-ribbed fruit colors fully and feels firm.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Support this Roman heirloom on a tall stake and prune for airflow in humid climates. The flattened, lightly ribbed red fruit is meaty and deeply flavored for slicing. Pick when the fruit colors fully and yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and set out plants after frost.

directions: Stake this Tuscan indeterminate and keep it well fed through a warm season. The ribbed, basket-shaped red fruit is meaty with few seeds and low acidity for cooking. Harvest when the pleated fruit ripens fully and softens at the base.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set out plants after frost.

directions: Support this indeterminate on a stake and prune for airflow around the fruit. The deeply ribbed, hollow yellow fruit has a pleated bell shape ideal for stuffing. Pick when the accordion-fluted fruit turns bright yellow and feels firm.

planting.note: Sow indoors and transplant once the last frost has passed.

directions: Stake this indeterminate and allow a long warm season for the hollow ribbed fruit to fill. The pleated, bell-shaped red fruit hollows out for stuffing and salads. Harvest when the fluted fruit colors fully and feels firm and light.

planting.note: Sow indoors and set out dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety forms a compact tree-type plant that suits large containers. Stake lightly and keep moisture consistent for the dusky purple fruit. Pick when the fruit takes on its full smoky-rose color and yields slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This stocky dwarf carries full-sized chocolate-brown fruit on a plant short enough for a pot. Provide a small stake and even watering to reduce cracking. Harvest when the fruit ripens to a deep brick-brown and softens slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out dwarf seedlings after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety carries dusky purple fruit on a compact, container-friendly plant. Provide a small stake and keep moisture consistent. Pick when the fruit deepens to a smoky purple-brown and gives to light pressure.

planting.note: Sow indoors and set out dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety bears green-when-ripe fruit with a bright, tangy flavor on a stocky plant. Stake lightly and judge ripeness by feel since the fruit stays green. Harvest when the fruit softens and shows a faint yellow-green blush.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety makes a compact plant with rich pink-red fruit and sweet flavor. Give it a light stake and even watering in a bed or large pot. Pick when the fruit colors to a deep crimson and softens slightly.

planting.note: Sow indoors and transplant dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This early dwarf project variety makes a compact plant well suited to short seasons and pots. Stake lightly and keep watering steady for the smooth pink fruit. Pick when the fruit colors to a soft rose-pink and gives slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out dwarf seedlings after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety produces dark purple-black fruit with rich flavor on a compact plant. Give it a small stake and even moisture through the season. Harvest when the fruit deepens to a dusky purple-brown and softens slightly.

planting.note: Sow indoors and set out dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety carries dark chocolate-mahogany fruit on a stocky, tree-type plant. Stake lightly and keep soil moisture consistent. Pick when the fruit takes on its full smoky color and yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety bears dark, multicolored fruit streaked with green and red on a compact plant. Provide a light stake and steady watering to reduce cracking. Harvest when the fruit deepens in color and gives to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: Sow indoors and plant out dwarf seedlings after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety combines a compact plant with heart-shaped dusky purple fruit. Stake lightly and keep moisture even to help the meaty hearts fill out. Pick when the fruit colors to a smoky purple and softens near the tip.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety carries pale yellow-gold fruit on a compact, container-friendly plant. Give a light stake and even moisture for the sweet, mild fruit. Harvest when the fruit turns a soft golden yellow and gives slightly.

planting.note: Sow indoors and transplant dwarf plants after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety produces dusky brick-ochre fruit with a rich flavor on a stocky plant. Stake lightly and keep the bed or pot evenly watered. Pick when the fruit ripens to its full muddy-orange color and softens gently.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out dwarf seedlings after frost.

directions: This dwarf project variety bears red fruit on a compact, tidy plant good for small gardens. Provide a light stake and keep watering steady. Harvest when the fruit colors fully red and yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: Start indoors and grow on in small pots after frost passes.

directions: This tidy micro-dwarf fits a small windowsill pot and carries heavy trusses of little fruit. Water evenly and keep it in the brightest spot available. Harvest the small red cherry fruit as it colors fully and softens.

planting.note: Start indoors and move pots and baskets outside after frost.

directions: This small trailing type is bred for window boxes, pots, and hanging baskets. Keep containers evenly moist and feed lightly for a steady flush of fruit. Pick the small red cherry fruit as it colors up and gives slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and grow in pots, setting out after frost.

directions: This dwarf bush type is ideal for small pots, boxes, and baskets on a sunny balcony. Keep the container evenly watered and give it full light for the best crop. Harvest the small red fruit as it colors fully and softens slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and move baskets outside after frost danger.

directions: This cascading type is bred to trail over the sides of hanging baskets and window boxes. Water baskets frequently since they dry fast, and feed regularly through the season. Pick the small red cherry fruit as it colors fully and gives to the touch.

planting.note: Start indoors and set baskets and pots out after frost.

directions: This trailing hybrid is well suited to hanging baskets and patio containers. Keep the mix consistently moist and feed lightly for a long run of fruit. Harvest the small red cherry fruit as it colors up and softens.

planting.note: Start indoors and move containers outdoors after frost.

directions: This semi-trailing hybrid produces heavily in baskets and large patio pots. Keep it evenly watered and fed, and let the stems spill over the container edge. Pick the small red cherry fruit as it colors fully and gives slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and move patio pots outside after frost.

directions: This compact determinate is bred for patio containers and small-space gardens. Give it a short cage or stake and keep the pot evenly moist. Harvest the small-to-medium red fruit as it colors fully and softens slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and grow in small pots year-round or set out after frost.

directions: This very small micro-dwarf thrives in a four-inch pot on a sunny windowsill and needs no support. Keep the little pot evenly moist and in strong light. Pick the tiny orange cherry fruit as it colors fully and softens.

planting.note: Start indoors and grow in small pots, moving out after frost.

directions: This tiny micro-dwarf carries pinkish-red fruit over silvery, compact foliage in a small pot. Keep the container evenly moist and in bright light for a good set. Harvest the small pink cherry fruit as it colors fully and gives slightly.

planting.note: Start indoors and grow in small pots, setting out after frost.

directions: This compact micro-dwarf fits small pots and window boxes and needs no staking. Keep the soil evenly moist and give it full light for a heavy set of little fruit. Pick the small red cherry fruit as it colors fully and softens.

planting.note: Plant out after nights are consistently mild.

directions: Sow indoors six to eight weeks ahead and transplant deeply once the ground warms. Give this vigorous indeterminate strong support and consistent moisture to carry its heavy fruit. The large green beefsteaks are ripe when they soften and show a faint amber tint at the blossom end.

planting.note: Transplant after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Begin seed indoors and set out hardened plants once the soil is warm, burying the stem to encourage rooting. Cage the indeterminate vines and keep watering steady to avoid cracking. The medium slicers ripen green with an emerald flesh, softening slightly and picking up a pale yellow blush when ready.

planting.note: Wait for settled warm weather before planting out.

directions: Start indoors under bright light and transplant deeply after frost. This tall indeterminate needs a sturdy cage or stake and even moisture through the season. Harvest the big green-gold fruits when they yield to light pressure and show a yellow undertone.

planting.note: Set out only after frost danger ends.

directions: Sow seed indoors and transplant when the soil has warmed, planting deep for strong roots. Support the indeterminate vines and mulch to hold soil moisture steady. Pick the medium emerald fruits when soft with a faint amber flush at the base.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Start indoors about six weeks before setting out and transplant deeply into warm soil. Provide the indeterminate vines strong support and steady water. The green slicers soften and pick up a light amber tone when fully ripe.

planting.note: Wait for warm settled weather before transplanting.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deeply after the last frost, spacing plants well for airflow. Cage or stake the vigorous indeterminate growth and water evenly to limit cracking. Harvest the large green fruits when they soften and take on a golden-green hue.

planting.note: Move to the garden after frost has ended.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the compact determinate plants out after frost, where they suit pots and small beds. A short cage keeps the fruit clean and the plant tidy. Pick the small green-gold fruits when they soften and glow lime-green from within.

planting.note: Transplant only once frost danger is fully past.

directions: Sow indoors six to eight weeks before your last frost and plant deep into warmed soil. This large indeterminate needs sturdy caging and even watering to support its heavy pale fruit. Harvest the big creamy-white beefsteaks when they soften and the skin turns ivory with a slight yellow cast.

planting.note: Set out after nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Begin seed indoors and transplant deeply once the soil warms in spring. Give the indeterminate vines strong support and shade the ripening fruit from harsh afternoon sun to prevent sunscald on the pale skin. Pick the round ivory-white fruits when they soften and reach a creamy color.

planting.note: Transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out deeply once frost has passed and soil has warmed. Cage the tall indeterminate vines and keep moisture even to reduce splitting. Harvest the creamy pale fruits when soft to the touch and blushed faintly yellow.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into warm soil, burying the stem to just below the lowest leaves. Support the indeterminate plant and protect the fruit from intense sun to keep the skin from scorching. The pale ivory fruits are ripe when they soften and turn creamy white.

planting.note: Set out after frost danger ends.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant deeply into warm soil once frost passes. The indeterminate vines are productive and need a strong cage to hold the clustered fruit. Harvest the egg-sized ivory tomatoes when they turn creamy pale yellow and feel slightly soft.

planting.note: Transplant after the soil warms in spring.

directions: Begin seed indoors and set out deep transplants after frost. Cage the indeterminate vines and provide some leaf cover for the fruit to avoid sunscald on the pale skin. The mild, low-acid fruits ripen to a soft creamy white and give slightly when pressed.

planting.note: Plant out only after all frost risk has passed.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deeply into warm soil after frost. Support the tall indeterminate vines and keep watering even to prevent the large fruit from splitting. Harvest the pale ivory-white beefsteaks when they soften and reach a creamy pastel color.

planting.note: Transplant once frost danger has ended.

directions: Start indoors and transplant deeply after frost into warm soil. This early, productive indeterminate sets well even in cooler summers and needs a cage or stake. Harvest the round citrus-yellow fruits when they color fully and give slightly to the touch.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and set them out deeply once frost passes and soil warms. Support the indeterminate vines and water evenly for smooth, crack-free fruit. Harvest the clear lemon-yellow slicers when they soften and reach a bright even gold.

planting.note: Transplant after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deeply into warm soil after frost, then cage the indeterminate vines. Pick frequently, since this heirloom is very productive over a long season. The small fruits have a soft fuzzy skin and are ripe when pale yellow flushed with a peachy-pink blush.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow indoors and set out deep transplants once frost has passed. Cage this tall indeterminate and feed it lightly to sustain its heavy yield. Pick the large yellow slicers when they soften and reach a bright lemon-gold color.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; ripens early.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost for an early crop, planting deep for strong roots. Cage the indeterminate vines and keep water steady through fruiting. Harvest the smooth round yellow fruits when they color fully and give slightly to the touch.

planting.note: Set out after frost danger passes.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost, giving the potato-leaf indeterminate a sturdy cage. This Siberian heirloom sets fruit well in cooler conditions and stays productive. Pick the round yellow cherries when they color fully and taste sweet.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant deeply after frost, supporting the indeterminate vines with a cage. The bell-pepper-shaped fruit is hollow and holds its shape well for stuffing. Harvest when the ribbed fruits turn fully bright yellow.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; needs a long warm season.

directions: Sow indoors early and transplant deeply into warm soil. This large beefsteak indeterminate needs strong staking and even feeding to carry its heavy fruit. Pick the big pale yellow beefsteaks when they soften and reach a smooth uniform gold.

planting.note: Set out after frost; a long-season variety.

directions: Start indoors early and transplant deeply once frost passes and soil warms. This large late indeterminate needs a strong cage and steady moisture to support its heavy yellow fruit. Harvest the big pale gold beefsteaks when they soften and color evenly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow indoors and set out deep transplants after frost. This semi-determinate variety stays fairly compact but still benefits from a cage. Harvest the round orange fruits when they color fully and give slightly to the touch.

planting.note: Set out after frost; long-season variety.

directions: Start indoors early and transplant deeply into warm soil. This large oxheart-type indeterminate needs strong support and even moisture for its heavy heart-shaped fruit. Harvest when the big orange fruits soften and color fully to a rich pumpkin-orange.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost, giving the productive indeterminate a tall cage. This French heirloom bears heavy clusters of golf-ball-sized fruit over a long season. Pick the round orange fruits when they deepen to a vivid flame-orange and give slightly.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost date.

directions: Start indoors and transplant after frost, caging the determinate plants for support. This variety is prized for its high beta-carotene content and mild flavor. Harvest the medium fruits when they color to a deep uniform orange and soften slightly.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant deeply into warm soil after frost. This vigorous indeterminate from New Zealand bears smooth round fruit and needs a strong cage. Harvest the deep orange globes when they color fully and give slightly to the touch.

planting.note: Transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Start indoors and transplant deeply into warm soil after frost, then cage the productive indeterminate vines. This meaty paste type bears heavy clusters and dries or sauces well. Harvest the elongated banana-shaped fruits when they turn a rich even orange.

planting.note: Set out after frost; long-season variety.

directions: Sow indoors early and transplant deeply into warm soil. This heart-shaped indeterminate needs strong support and steady moisture for its large meaty fruit. Harvest when the big orange hearts soften and color evenly.

planting.note: Set out after frost; needs a long warm season.

directions: Sow indoors early and transplant deeply into warm soil once frost passes. This large indeterminate needs strong support and steady moisture to carry its big fruit. Harvest the beefsteaks when they turn green and amber outside with rosy purple-green marbling inside and soften slightly.

planting.note: Set hardened transplants out after all frost danger has passed; start seed indoors six to eight weeks earlier.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm cell trays and grow on until stocky before transplanting deeply, burying the lower stem to root out. Stake or cage this compact determinate for support and water evenly to guard against blossom end rot. The meaty, low-juice plum fruit ripens all at once, making a single sauce harvest easy.

planting.note: Move plants outside after the last spring frost and warm soil.

directions: Begin seed indoors and harden off gradually before transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. This vigorous indeterminate needs tall caging or trellising and steady moisture to fill its oversized paste fruit. Harvest the big blocky plums when deep red for a heavy, meaty sauce yield.

planting.note: Transplant after danger of frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Start seed in warm trays and set the stocky transplants deep in fertile soil. This bushy determinate needs only light staking and rewards even moisture with a concentrated crop. Gather the crack-resistant red fruit as it colors for reliable canning and sauce.

planting.note: A short-season paste type; transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Sow indoors and grow compact transplants for setting out after frost, favoring this variety in cool northern gardens. The low, spreading determinate ripens its whole crop early, so mulch well to keep fruit off the soil. Harvest the oval red plums together for fast sauce making before autumn.

planting.note: Set out after frost into warm, well-drained ground.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant sturdy seedlings deeply after the soil warms. This disease-resistant determinate holds fruit well and benefits from a cage and steady watering. Pick the firm, sweet plum tomatoes as they redden for sauce, paste, and fresh use.

planting.note: Transplant after all frost danger passes.

directions: Grow seed indoors and set out stocky plants deeply once nights stay mild. Support the determinate bushes and keep watering even to prevent cracking on the plum fruit. Harvest the elongated red tomatoes as they color for a heavy sauce and salsa crop.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors after the last frost date.

directions: Start indoors and transplant hardened seedlings into fertile, well-drained beds. This blight-tolerant determinate stands up to wet seasons and appreciates a cage plus mulch. Collect the smooth red plums at full color for dependable sauce even in disease-prone regions.

planting.note: Transplant into the tunnel or garden after frost; well suited to protected culture.

directions: Sow indoors and train these vigorous indeterminate plants up strings in a greenhouse or high tunnel. Prune to one or two leaders, remove suckers, and feed regularly for a long picking window. Harvest the firm, disease-resistant plum fruit continuously through the season.

planting.note: An early paste tomato; transplant after frost, ideal for short seasons and containers.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the compact plants out after frost, in beds or large pots. This early, tidy determinate needs little support and ripens quickly in cool climates. Pick the bright orange-red plums as they color for fast early-season sauce.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant Czech heirloom that can go out early once hard frost has passed.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant early, as this hardy variety sets fruit in cool weather better than most. Cage the potato-leaf indeterminate vines and keep them evenly watered. Pick the small, sweet red fruit continuously from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Cold-set variety suited to early transplanting in short-season areas.

directions: Start seed indoors and set the compact semi-determinate plants out early with cloche protection if needed. This cool-tolerant type fruits reliably in chilly springs and needs only light support. Harvest the small red fruit early and often for fresh eating and salads.

planting.note: Bred for cool, wet Pacific Northwest springs; transplant after frost.

directions: Grow indoors and set stocky plants out after frost, favoring this blight-tolerant determinate in cool coastal gardens. It sets fruit parthenocarpically in chilly weather and needs only a small cage. Harvest the large, round red slicers as they ripen in a concentrated flush.

planting.note: Cold-tolerant early determinate; can go out under protection soon after frost.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant early, as this variety sets nearly seedless fruit in cool spring weather. The compact determinate needs little support and does well in short seasons. Pick the medium red fruit as it ripens for an early, dependable harvest.

planting.note: Sets fruit in cool weather; transplant early in short-season gardens.

directions: Start indoors and set out the compact determinate plants early, using row cover in cold snaps. This Russian variety fruits at temperatures that stall other tomatoes. Harvest the small to medium red fruit from early summer through the season.

planting.note: One of the earliest tomatoes; ideal for the shortest cool seasons.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant the very compact determinate plants out early with light protection. Developed for cold prairie climates, it sets a heavy early cluster of fruit on small plants. Pick the small red tomatoes as soon as they color for the earliest crop possible.

planting.note: Cold-tolerant Russian heirloom; transplant after frost, tolerates cool nights.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant early, as this variety tolerates cool weather well. Cage the indeterminate vines and water evenly for smooth, crack-resistant fruit. Harvest the deep red, richly flavored globes early and continuously through summer.

planting.note: Siberian heirloom that fruits in cool climates; transplant after frost.

directions: Grow seedlings indoors and set them out early, since this variety yields well in cool, short seasons. Support the indeterminate plants with a cage and keep moisture steady. Pick the round red fruit early for a reliable crop where summers are brief.

planting.note: Early Russian determinate suited to containers and cool seasons.

directions: Sow indoors and set out the compact plants early, prizing them for lacy, carrot-like silvery foliage. This ornamental determinate thrives in pots and cool gardens with minimal staking. Harvest the medium red fruit in an early concentrated flush for fresh use.

planting.note: Early orange determinate bred for cold northern gardens.

directions: Start indoors and transplant the compact determinate plants out early in the season. It sets fruit reliably in cool weather and needs little support. Pick the bright orange fruit as it ripens in an early, abundant crop.

planting.note: Siberian beefsteak that sizes fruit in cool summers; transplant after frost.

directions: Grow indoors and set out early, as this large-fruited heirloom performs in cool short seasons. Cage the indeterminate vines and water deeply to fill the big fruit. Harvest the sweet, pink-red beefsteaks when fully colored for slicing.

planting.note: A leading greenhouse hybrid; transplant into protected culture or the garden after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors and train the vigorous indeterminate up a string in a greenhouse or high tunnel. Prune to a single leader, remove suckers, and feed steadily for long production. Harvest the uniform, crack-resistant red slicers continuously as trusses ripen.

planting.note: Greenhouse beefsteak hybrid; grow under cover for best results.

directions: Sow indoors and train the tall indeterminate plants up strings in a protected house. Prune to one leader, feed well, and manage humidity for disease-free trusses. Harvest the large, smooth red beefsteaks continuously through a long greenhouse season.

planting.note: Classic greenhouse cluster hybrid; transplant into protected culture after frost.

directions: Start indoors and train the indeterminate vines up support in a greenhouse or tunnel. Prune to a single stem, remove side shoots, and feed regularly for even trusses. Harvest the firm, glossy red cluster fruit as each truss colors uniformly.

subcategory: fruiting; early pink heirloom beefsteak tomato

planting.note: Earlier beefsteak; transplant after frost.

directions: Grow indoors and transplant the potato-leaf plants deep after frost, then cage them. Water steadily and feed moderately for large, meaty fruit early in the season. Harvest the pink-purple beefsteaks at full color for a rich, brandywine-like flavor.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm, fertile ground.

directions: Start indoors and set out after frost, caging the vigorous indeterminate vines. Water evenly for smooth, blemish-free, crack-resistant fruit. Harvest the round red globes when fully colored for a balanced, dependable slicing tomato.

planting.note: A record-holding superhot cross that needs a very early indoor start for a full crop.

directions: Keep seed warm and consistently moist while waiting out slow germination. Provide full sun and steady feeding through a long growing season. Harvest the wrinkled, blistered red pods with gloves, as heat is extreme.

planting.note: This Andean species tolerates cooler nights than most peppers but needs an extremely long season.

directions: Start the black seed very early, as germination and cropping are both slow. Plants are sprawling, hairy-leaved, and can overwinter in mild areas or a greenhouse. Pick the thick-walled, apple-shaped fruit when fully red, orange, or yellow.

planting.note: A selected extra-hot habanero that ripens late, so begin indoors two to three months ahead.

directions: Germinate seed in steady warmth and grow on in a hot, sunny position. Feed lightly and water evenly to keep the heavy fruit set developing. Harvest the plump, wrinkled pods once they turn deep glossy red.

planting.note: Begin indoors and set out after the soil warms for a heavy summer crop.

directions: Start seed in warmth and transplant vigorous seedlings into full sun. This productive selection carries long, slender pods well above the foliage. Pick the fruit green or let it ripen to glossy red for maximum heat.

planting.note: Slow to start, so sow well ahead and transplant only into thoroughly warm soil.

directions: Germinate seed in consistent warmth and grow the bushy plants in full sun. The tiny, upright pods pack intense heat and are the base of piri piri sauce. Harvest when they turn bright red and pick often to keep the plant producing.

planting.note: This wild-type chile germinates slowly and needs an early indoor start.

directions: Soak and warm the seed to improve slow, erratic germination. Plants form open, woody bushes that tolerate heat and lean soil well. Harvest the tiny oval pods when bright red for a sharp, quick heat.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for a steady summer picking crop.

directions: Sow in warm trays and grow the plants in full sun with even moisture. Pick the small green pods young when only about an inch and a half long, when most are mild. Left on the plant they grow larger, hotter, and eventually ripen red.

planting.note: This Peruvian baccatum needs a long season, so sow indoors well in advance.

directions: Germinate seed in warmth and grow the tall, spreading plants with support. Fruit ripens from green to a deep brownish red with a mild, berry-like heat. Harvest when fully colored, as this chile is often dried for Peruvian cooking.

planting.note: A Chilean baccatum best started indoors for a full late-summer harvest.

directions: Sow in warm conditions and grow the productive plants in full sun. The tapered pods are picked pale green or allowed to ripen to red. Frequent harvest of the crisp, fruity chiles keeps the plant setting more fruit.

planting.note: Begin indoors and move out after frost for this hotter New Mexico type.

directions: Germinate in warmth and grow transplants in full sun with even watering. The tapered pods offer more heat than most green chile varieties. Harvest green for roasting, or let them turn red for ristras and grinding.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for a heavy, uniform chile crop.

directions: Sow warm and grow the reliable, productive plants in full sun. This improved Anaheim-type sets uniform, meaty pods with mild to medium heat. Pick green for roasting or allow to ripen red for a sweeter flavor.

planting.note: This long-season Mexican chile is best started indoors weeks before transplanting.

directions: Germinate in warmth and grow the tall plants in full sun with steady moisture. The long, slender pods are known fresh as chilaca and dried as pasilla. Harvest when they ripen from dark green to deep chocolate brown.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for this poblano-type chile.

directions: Sow in warm trays and grow the sturdy plants in full sun. This poblano relative ripens to a rich chocolate brown rather than red. Dry the mature pods for a deep, mild chile used in traditional mole.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for a colorful ornamental edible.

directions: Sow warm and grow the compact, purple-tinged plants in full sun. Upright pods pass through purple, yellow, orange, and red all at once for a rainbow effect. The small fruit is edible and quite hot, and the plant suits containers.

planting.note: Begin indoors and transplant after frost for this colorful compact pepper.

directions: Sow warm and grow the neat, rounded plants in full sun or a bright container. Tiny upright pods ripen through purple, yellow, orange, and red for a multicolored display. The fruit is edible and hot, and heavy fruiting makes it very decorative.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for this dark-leaved ornamental.

directions: Sow warm and grow in full sun to bring out the near-black foliage. Round pods emerge glossy black and ripen to bright red like small pearls. The fruit is edible and hot, and the plant is prized as a bedding ornamental.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for a multicolored edible ornamental.

directions: Sow warm and grow the tidy plants in full sun or a container. Small upright pods display purple, cream, yellow, orange, and red simultaneously. The fruit is edible and quite hot, making this both decorative and useful.

planting.note: Begin indoors and transplant after frost for this blue-fruited ornamental.

directions: Sow warm and grow in full sun to deepen the purple-blue pod color. Small round fruit begins an unusual blue and ripens through purple to red. The peppers are edible with a moderate heat, and the plant thrives in containers.

planting.note: This tall baccatum needs a long season, so sow indoors well in advance.

directions: Germinate in warmth and give the very tall plants sturdy staking. Fruit is fragrant and fruity with a bright, lingering heat. Harvest the elongated pods when they ripen to a soft peach color, and pick often for a huge yield.

planting.note: A seasoning-type chinense that ripens late, so begin seed early indoors.

directions: Germinate seed in steady warmth and grow in a hot, sunny spot. This habanero relative offers the fruity aroma without significant heat. Harvest the small lantern pods once they ripen to golden yellow.

planting.note: A heatless habanero best started indoors two to three months before transplanting.

directions: Germinate warm and grow the plants in full sun with even moisture. This bred selection delivers full habanero aroma and sweetness without the burn. Harvest the small tapered pods when they ripen to bright orange.

planting.note: This Brazilian chinense ripens late, so sow indoors well ahead of transplanting.

directions: Germinate seed in warmth and grow in a hot, sunny position. Tiny teardrop pods with a pointed beak carry a smoky, fruity flavor and mild heat. Harvest when bright red, when the fruit is often pickled whole.

planting.note: This Brazilian baccatum wants a long season and an early indoor sowing.

directions: Sow warm and grow the tall, branching plants with support. The slender, finger-shaped pods offer a moderate, fruity heat. Harvest when ripened to bright red, when they are used fresh or dried into flakes.

planting.note: A slow-growing Amazonian chinense that needs the earliest possible start.

directions: Germinate seed in warmth and expect a slow, uneven start. Grow in a hot, sunny spot with steady moisture through a long season. Harvest the tiny round yellow pods, which carry an intense aroma and sharp heat.

planting.note: This baccatum needs a long warm season, so sow indoors well ahead.

directions: Sow warm and grow the productive plants in full sun. Small tapered pods deliver a bright, fruity flavor with moderate heat. Harvest when they ripen to golden yellow-orange for the fullest taste.

planting.note: Begin this colorful baccatum indoors for a full late-season crop.

directions: Sow warm and grow the ornamental plants in full sun. Pods pass through cream, purple, yellow, and orange to red as they ripen. The fruit is edible with a fruity, medium heat, and the shifting colors are decorative.

planting.note: This South African baccatum ripens late, so sow indoors well in advance.

directions: Germinate in warmth and grow the plants in full sun with even watering. Small round pods ripen bright red with a sweet, mildly hot, tangy flavor. Harvest when fully colored, when the little peppers are often pickled whole.

planting.note: Start this Middle Eastern chile indoors weeks before transplanting.

directions: Sow in warm trays and grow the plants in full sun with steady moisture. The tapered pods ripen deep red and carry a fruity, moderate heat. Dry and coarsely grind the mature fruit for the classic crushed pepper flake.

planting.note: This Turkish chile needs a warm season, so begin seed indoors early.

directions: Sow warm and grow the plants in full sun with even moisture. Pods ripen to a very dark maroon and are traditionally sun-dried and sweated. The resulting flake is smoky, raisin-like, and mildly hot.

planting.note: Start this Turkish chile indoors and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow warm and grow the plants in full sun with regular water. The pods ripen dark red with a fruity, moderately hot flavor. Sun-dry and crush the mature fruit into a coarse, oily flake for cooking.

planting.note: Start this New Mexico landrace indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow warm and grow this heirloom landrace in full sun with lean, well-drained soil. Pods are slender and somewhat irregular with a variable moderate heat. Harvest green for roasting or let them ripen red for drying into ristras.

planting.note: An early-maturing chile good for shorter seasons, started indoors before frost.

directions: Sow warm and grow in full sun; this variety ripens earlier than most New Mexico chiles. Pods carry a medium heat and color up quickly, suiting cooler regions. Pick green for roasting or let them turn red for drying.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for this hottest New Mexico type.

directions: Sow warm and grow the productive plants in full sun with rich soil. This is among the hottest of the New Mexico green chiles, with long tapering pods. Harvest green for roasting, or let the fruit ripen red for a sweeter, hotter flavor.

planting.note: Start this Mexican chile indoors weeks before transplanting into warm soil.

directions: Sow warm and grow the plants in full sun with regular water. The slender pods resemble a smaller, hotter guajillo with a fruity, sharp heat. Harvest when they ripen deep red, then dry for sauces and salsas.

planting.note: A very hot chinense that demands the earliest possible indoor start.

directions: Germinate seed in warmth and be patient through slow, staggered sprouting. Grow in full sun with rich soil and steady moisture over a long season. Harvest the wrinkled red pods with gloves, as heat comes on with delay and intensity.

planting.note: This South American baccatum wants a long season and an early start.

directions: Sow warm and grow the plants in full sun with even moisture. The small, flattened, ridged pods have a fruity flavor and moderate heat. Harvest when ripened to red, orange, or yellow depending on the strain.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for this productive Asian chile.

directions: Sow warm and grow the vigorous plants in full sun. Long, curving pods resemble a horn and carry a cayenne-level heat. Pick green for stir-fries or let the fruit ripen bright red for drying.

planting.note: An early cayenne good for shorter seasons, started indoors before frost.

directions: Sow warm and grow the compact plants in full sun; this cayenne matures early and heavily. The slim pods ripen quickly to bright red with a sharp heat. Pick often to keep the plant producing throughout the season.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for this exceptionally long cayenne.

directions: Sow warm and grow the plants in full sun with even moisture. This heirloom produces remarkably long, slender, curling pods. Harvest bright red for drying, when the thin walls make it easy to grind into powder.

planting.note: Start this heirloom indoors and transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow warm and grow the ornamental plants in full sun. Slender pods emerge a striking purple and ripen through orange to deep red. The fruit carries a cayenne-like heat and the color shift makes the plant decorative.

planting.note: Begin this Spanish pepper indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow warm and grow the plants in full sun with even moisture. The small, tapering pods ripen bright red with a sweet, mild flavor. Harvest when fully red, when they are traditionally roasted and peeled.

planting.note: This Brazilian chinense ripens late, so start seed indoors well ahead.

directions: Germinate seed in warmth and grow the plants in a hot, sunny spot. The small round pods carry a strong aroma and a distinct fruity heat. Harvest when ripened to red or yellow depending on the strain.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for this Chinese cooking chile.

directions: Sow warm and grow the upright plants in full sun. Small, slender pods point outward and ripen to bright red with a clean, sharp heat. Harvest when red and dry for use in Sichuan and Hunan cooking.

planting.note: This tall baccatum needs a long season, so sow indoors well ahead.

directions: Germinate in warmth and stake the very tall, productive plants. Elongated pods ripen with peach and red striping and a sweet, fruity heat. Harvest frequently once the fruit shows its stripes to keep the crop coming.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and move outdoors a week or two after the last spring frost.

directions: Start seed in warm conditions and set hardened plants into fertile ground once the soil has warmed. Harvest the small green fruits at about one to two inches for tapas frying, when most stay mild but the occasional one turns hot. Frequent picking keeps flowers setting through the season.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors six to eight weeks before the last frost and transplant into warm soil.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm trays and set plants out after all danger of frost has passed. Harvest the wrinkled yellow-green fruits at two to three inches for pickling, when they are barely warm and slightly sweet. Regular picking encourages a long, heavy set.

planting.note: Start seed very early indoors as this species is slow and prefers cooler nights than most peppers.

directions: Sow early and grow the hairy-leaved, sprawling plants on for a long season, as they tolerate cooler weather better than other peppers. Pick the thick, apple-shaped fruits with their distinctive black seeds when fully red, orange, or yellow. Given protection the plant can overwinter and crop for several years.

planting.note: Start indoors six to eight weeks early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Set warm-grown transplants into fertile ground once the soil has warmed. The long, slender fruits are the fresh chilaca chile when dark green and become dried pasilla when ripened deep brown. Let fruits color fully before drying for the classic raisin-like flavor.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and move them out after frost danger passes.

directions: Grow transplants on in warmth and set into rich soil once nights are mild. This poblano-type ripens to a deep chocolate brown rather than red and dries into the sweet, smoky mulato chile. Allow the heart-shaped fruits to color fully for the richest flavor.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow seed warm and move hardened transplants outdoors after frost. This award-winning hybrid produces large, elongated fruits that grill beautifully and turn from green to red. Pick at either stage and harvest regularly to keep the vigorous plants bearing.

planting.note: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks before setting plants out after frost.

directions: Raise transplants in warmth and plant into rich, well-drained soil once nights are mild. This Italian heirloom bears large, boxy four-lobed fruits that ripen from green to golden yellow. Let them color fully for the sweetest, thick-walled flesh.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow warm and set transplants out after frost into fertile ground. The pointed fruits ripen from creamy white through orange to red and are sweet at every stage. Dry the fully red fruits and grind them for homemade sweet paprika.

planting.note: Start indoors six to eight weeks early and transplant into warm soil.

directions: Sow warm and set plants into fertile ground after frost. This Bulgarian heirloom bears long, tapered fruits that ripen to a deep red and are prized for roasting and canning. Let the horns color fully for their sweet, rich flavor.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Start seed in warmth and transplant into rich soil once nights are mild. This heirloom pimento produces squat, thick-walled fruits shaped like a sheep's nose that ripen to deep red. Harvest fully red for the sweetest flesh, ideal for roasting and canning.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow warm and set compact transplants into fertile ground after frost. The blocky, tapered fruits ripen from pale yellow to bright red and are sweet and juicy for frying. Harvest at the yellow stage for a mild flavor or leave to redden for sweetness.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Raise transplants in warmth and plant into rich soil once frost has passed. This Chicago Italian heirloom bears glossy, tapered fruits prized for frying, ripening green to red. Pick often at either stage to keep the plants producing all season.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow warm and set the compact plants into fertile ground after frost. This award-winning variety bears cone-shaped fruits that ripen from creamy yellow through rose to red with mild, sweet-hot flavor. The heavy set on small plants makes it well suited to containers.

planting.note: Start seed early indoors and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Germinate warm and give the plants a long season before and after transplanting. The tapered fruits ripen from green to a deep burgundy-brown and carry mild heat with berry-like flavor. Dry the ripe fruits for the smoky seasoning used in Peruvian cooking.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow warm and set transplants into fertile ground after frost. This heirloom cayenne produces exceptionally long, curling red fruits that dry easily for grinding or stringing. Harvest fully red and hang the thin-walled pods to dry.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow warm and set the compact, ornamental plants into fertile soil after frost. The jalapeno-shaped fruits ripen from green through deep purple to red, making the plant attractive as well as productive. Pick at the green or purple stage for a classic jalapeno bite.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow warm and set transplants into fertile ground after frost. This yellow jalapeno type ripens from bright yellow through orange to red with moderate heat and disease resistance. Harvest at the yellow stage for color or leave to ripen for a sweeter, hotter fruit.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow warm and transplant into fertile soil once nights are mild. This mild jalapeno bred at Texas A&M offers full jalapeno flavor with much reduced heat, ripening green to red. Pick green for stuffing and fresh use, or let redden for a touch more warmth.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow warm and set transplants into fertile ground after frost. This improved jalapeno bears extra-large, thick-walled fruits well suited to stuffing and slicing. Harvest green and firm, or leave a few to redden for extra sweetness and heat.

planting.note: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks early and transplant after frost.

directions: Sow warm and set transplants into rich soil after frost. This Italian tomato-shaped cheese pepper bears squat, thick-walled fruits that ripen to glossy red with very sweet flesh. Let them color fully for stuffing, pickling, or fresh eating.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Sow warm and set the compact plants into fertile soil after frost. The small, blocky fruits ripen to bright orange with thick, exceptionally sweet flesh perfect for snacking. Pick often once orange to encourage a heavy, continuous set.

planting.note: Start indoors six to eight weeks early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Sow warm and set transplants into rich, well-drained soil after frost. This hybrid ripens from green to a glowing golden yellow with thick, very sweet walls. Let the blocky fruits color fully for the richest flavor, and support heavy-bearing plants.

planting.note: A Southern heirloom bred for hot, humid summers.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant into fertile soil once conditions turn dependably warm. The tall, disease-tolerant plants bear large, glossy dark purple fruit of classic teardrop shape. Harvest when the skin still gleams and springs back lightly to the touch.

planting.note: An early hybrid good for shorter-season gardens.

directions: Start indoors and transplant early-maturing plants into warm ground after the last frost. Compact plants yield glossy, deep purple-black fruit of medium size well before summer's end. Pick the fruit while firm and shining for the best texture.

planting.note: A green-skinned hybrid with an elongated teardrop shape.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into warm soil after frost has fully cleared. Vigorous plants set a good run of glossy, jade-green teardrop fruit. Cut fruit while the skin is bright and taut for tender, mild flesh.

planting.note: A vigorous hybrid bred for high yields and disease tolerance.

directions: Start indoors and transplant strong plants into warm, fertile soil after the last frost. Large, glossy purple-black teardrop fruit set early and continue over a long run. Harvest when the skin is firm and shining and springs back to a gentle press.

planting.note: A large-fruited hybrid favored for grilling and slicing.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant into warm, rich soil once the weather settles. Big, oval, deep purple fruit grow on productive, upright plants that benefit from staking. Harvest while glossy for broad, low-seed slices ideal for the grill.

planting.note: A white-fruited hybrid with large, teardrop fruit.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into warm soil after the last frost has passed. Vigorous plants bear large, pure-white, teardrop fruit with mild, creamy flesh. Harvest while the white skin is glossy, before it dulls and the fruit turns bitter.

planting.note: A bright magenta-pink hybrid with tender fruit.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into warm ground once frost danger has passed. Plants set glowing pink-magenta, oval fruit prized for their thin skin and sweet flesh. Harvest while the color is vivid and the skin glossy for the best eating quality.

planting.note: An Italian-type hybrid noted for early, heavy yields.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant vigorous plants into warm, fertile soil after frost. Long, glossy dark purple fruit set early and keep coming from strong, upright plants. Harvest while the fruit is firm and shining for tender, mild flesh.

planting.note: A French-bred hybrid with classic glossy black fruit.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into warm soil once the season is dependably mild. Well-branched plants carry rounded-oval, deep purple-black fruit over a long harvest. Cut fruit while glossy and firm for smooth, mild flesh.

planting.note: An Italian-type hybrid with long, cylindrical fruit.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into warm, fertile soil after frost danger passes. Productive plants bear long, glossy, deep purple cylindrical fruit over a long run. Harvest while firm and shining for tender flesh with few seeds.

planting.note: An early large-fruited hybrid reliable in many climates.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant into warm, rich soil once frost danger has cleared. Large, glossy, deep purple oval fruit set early on dependable, vigorous plants. Harvest while the skin is bright and firm for mild, tender flesh.

planting.note: A French heirloom producing large, round fruit.

directions: Start indoors well ahead and transplant into fertile, warm soil after frost. Big, round, deep purple fruit ripen on strong plants in a long, hot season. Harvest while glossy for dense, creamy flesh well suited to stuffing and roasting.

planting.note: A rose-and-white striped heirloom-type for warm gardens.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant into warm, rich soil after the last frost. Plump, oval fruit ripen a soft rose streaked and shaded with creamy white. Harvest while the striping is bright and the skin glossy for mild, tender flesh.

planting.note: An Indian-type hybrid with purple-and-white streaked fruit.

directions: Start indoors and transplant compact plants into warm ground after frost. Small, teardrop fruit are painted purple over white and can be picked baby or larger. Harvest early and often while the streaking is bright for sweet, tender fruit.

planting.note: A heat-loving African relative grown for its bright fruit.

directions: Start indoors early since germination can be slow, then transplant into hot, fertile soil. Tall plants carry small, ribbed fruit that ripen from green through orange to red. Harvest at the green-to-orange stage for mild eating, or leave to redden for a bolder, ornamental fruit.

planting.note: Plant after frost when soil reaches at least 65 degrees.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep in a row of loose, fertile soil and thin to a few inches apart. This dependable old bush type produces smooth, stringless round pods that stay tender in hot weather. Gather pods every couple of days to stretch the harvest window.

planting.note: Sow directly after danger of frost passes.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm soil and firm them in gently. Topcrop is an early, heavy-yielding award winner with straight green stringless pods. Keep the vines picked clean and they will often set a light second flush.

planting.note: Sow at a support once soil is warm and frost-free.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep against a strong trellis or wigwam and water generously through summer. This popular British runner throws masses of scarlet blooms followed by long, flavorful green pods. Keep picking the pods young to encourage a long, heavy harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep the bed moist through germination. Red Swan carries unusual dusty rose-pink pods with a rich, buttery snap-bean flavor. Pick the pods young while the color is brightest and the texture most tender.

planting.note: Sow at the base of a trellis after frost.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep beside a tall support and let the strong vines climb. Lavender flowers give way to deep purple pods that are easy to find among green leaves. Pick often while young and the plants will keep bearing until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow at a support once frost has passed.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep by a trellis; this old French heirloom tolerates heat and drought well. Purple-tinged foliage sets off dusky blue-purple pods that hold cinnamon-colored beans inside. Use pods young as snap beans or let them mature for shelling and drying.

common_name: Pencil Pod Black Wax Bush Bean

planting.note: Direct sow after frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm ground and firm the soil over them. This heirloom wax bean bears bright yellow, round, stringless pods over black seeds. Pick the pencil-thick pods young and often for tender, buttery snap beans.

common_name: Top Notch Golden Wax Bush Bean

planting.note: Sow directly once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow an inch deep in a sunny row and thin to a few inches apart. This compact bush yields flat, golden-yellow stringless pods early in the season. Keep the pods picked to maintain steady production through summer.

planting.note: Direct sow at a support after frost.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep beside a trellis and let the climbing vines run up it. This heirloom bears tan beans streaked with brown, named for their goat-eye markings. Leave the pods to dry on the plant, then shell the beans for hearty stews.

planting.note: Sow after frost; needs a long warm season to finish.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil and grow through a long summer. This California heirloom produces small, firm pink beans famous in Santa Maria-style barbecue. Let the pods dry on the plant before shelling out the compact beans.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost with a low support.

directions: Sow an inch deep and let the trailing half-runner vines ramble or climb a short trellis. Appaloosa produces distinctive white beans splashed with brown-black at one end. Leave pods to dry fully on the plant, then shell the beans for winter soups.

planting.note: Sow at a tall support after the last frost.

directions: Plant the large seeds an inch or two deep at a sturdy support and water well in dry spells. Czar is a white-flowered runner bearing long green pods and plump white shelling beans. Pick pods young to eat as snaps, or leave them to fill out for large white beans.

planting.note: Sow at a trellis once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep against a strong support and keep well watered through summer. Sunset carries soft salmon-pink flowers followed by long, tender green pods. Harvest pods young and often to keep the ornamental vines producing.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil at a support after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in thoroughly warm soil at the base of a tall support. This Southern heirloom shows narrow willow-like leaves and bears small, sweet baby lima beans. Shell the beans when the pods plump, or let them dry for storage.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil at a trellis after frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in warm ground beside a sturdy pole or trellis. This old Southern pole lima bears small white flat baby limas over a long, hot season. Pick the pods when well filled for shelling, or leave them to dry on the vine.

planting.note: Sow at a trellis into hot soil after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil at a tall support and let the vigorous vines climb. This heat-loving Southern heirloom keeps setting speckled buff-and-maroon butterbeans through summer. Shell the pods when plump, or let them dry fully for storage.

planting.note: Sow at a support after the last frost.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep at a trellis and let the strong vines climb. This German heirloom bears long, stringless purple pods that turn green when cooked. Its purple flowers and pods stand out clearly, making picking easy and fast.

planting.note: Direct sow at a support after frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep at the base of a trellis and let the vines climb. Cobra sets long, straight, round green pods over a long season with good disease resistance. Pick the smooth pods young and often to keep the vines cropping.

planting.note: Sow directly once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny row and thin to a few inches apart. Rocdor is a French filet-type wax bean with slender, bright yellow, stringless pods. Pick the thin pods young for the finest texture and keep harvesting to prolong the crop.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and keep the bed evenly moist. Sonesta forms compact bushes with smooth, golden-yellow, tender stringless pods. Gather the pods young and frequently for the sweetest, most buttery flavor.

planting.note: Sow after frost, traditionally among tall corn.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep at a strong support or among sturdy cornstalks in the old way. This heat-tolerant heirloom, also called Striped Creaseback, bears round creased green pods. Harvest the pods young and tender for snap beans through a long summer.

planting.note: Sow after frost for a dry baking-bean crop.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in a sunny bed and keep early weeds under control. This classic New England baking bean is white with a golden-tan eye around the seed scar. Allow the pods to dry on the plant, then shell out the beans for winter use.

planting.note: Direct sow once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep in warm soil and firm them in place. Empress sets a heavy crop of long, straight, dark green stringless pods held above the foliage. Pick the pods young and often for tender snap beans and continued production.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost.

directions: Sow an inch deep in warm soil and thin seedlings a few inches apart. Venture produces long, round, dark green stringless pods on compact upright plants. Keep the pods picked young to maintain tenderness and steady yield.

planting.note: Sow at a support after frost.

directions: Set seeds an inch deep beside a trellis and let the vines climb. This heirloom is prized for a mushroom-like flavor and yellow beans used fresh or dried. Pick pods young as snaps, or leave them to mature and dry for shelling.

planting.note: Direct sow at a support after frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at a tall trellis and let the vigorous vines climb. This family heirloom bears long, flat Italian romano-type pods over a long season. Harvest the meaty pods while tender for the richest flavor.

planting.note: Sow directly after the last frost.

directions: Plant seeds an inch deep in a sunny row and keep the soil evenly damp. This French heirloom wax bean tolerates cool soil and yields bright yellow, tender pods. Pick the pods young and often for buttery flavor and continued cropping.

planting.note: Sow at a support after the last frost.

directions: Sow seeds an inch deep at a trellis and let the vines climb. This German heirloom bears long, golden-yellow, stringless pods that are easy to spot and pick. Harvest the pods young for tender wax beans throughout the season.
